title: The Symbol That Raises the Soul — Iamblichus and the Divine Work Hidden in Matter
author: Iamblichus / Thomas Taylor

body.text: Iamblichus begins where Porphyry hesitates. How can asoul buried in generation reach what is above soul? The answer is theurgy: divine work. The god has already placed signatures throughout the world. The soul ascends by learning how to touch them.

Hence the Egyptians, perceiving that all superior natures rejoice in the similitude to them of inferior beings, and thus wishing to fill the latter with good, through the greatest possible imitation of the former, very properly exhibit amode of theologizing adapted to the mystic doctrine concealed in the symbols.

This is the first law of theurgy. The lower resembles the higher because the higher has impressed itself into the lower like aseal into wax. Astone, herb, name, or gesture is chosen because it bears alikeness. The symbol reveals connection.

Hear, therefore, the intellectual interpretation of symbols, according to the conceptions of the Egyptians; at the same time removing from your imagination and your ears the image of things symbolical, but elevating yourself to intellectual truth.

The outer image is only the beginning. The symbol must first be seen, then raised. If the eye stays at the literal surface, it meets only surface. If it becomes intellectual, the same stone becomes agate.

body.caption: The gate — the symbol as passage between visible and invisible.

body.text: For man, as these writings say, has two souls. And one, indeed, is derived from the first intelligible, and participates of the power of the Demiurgus; but the other is imparted from the circulation of the celestial bodies, to which the soul that sees God returns.
One part of the soul descends through the celestial spheres. Another remains with the intelligible. Astrology maps the field of descent. Theurgy teaches return.
Nor do theurgists disturb the divine intellect about trifling concerns; but they consult it about things which pertain to the purification, liberation, and salvation of the soul.
Theurgy concerns purification, liberation, and salvation. It shifts the soul'sallegiance from generated identity to divine origin. The symbol begins in matter. The operation ends beyond birth.

body.caption: The symbol as vehicle — beginning in matter, ending beyond the world.

body.text: Such theurgy, likewise, as leads to an unbegotten nature is perfected conformably to alife of this kind.
Theurgy leads toward the unbegotten. It transforms the soul'sground. The world becomes atemple when desire is purified enough to use it rightly.
